title: Shrewsbury biscuits
subtitle: English butter biscuits with lemon zest and currants
excerpt: One of England's oldest named biscuits: a crisp, buttery round flavoured with lemon zest and dotted with currants. Recipes appear in English cookbooks from the seventeenth century. The texture is firm and short, somewhere between a shortbread and a ginger snap — short from the butter but with more bite from the egg and sugar.
sourceNotes: Shrewsbury cakes and biscuits are among the oldest documented English biscuits, with recipes appearing in sources from the seventeenth century onwards. Elinor Fettiplace's Receipt Book (c.1604, published by Hilary Spurling, 1986) includes an early version. Later recipes appear in Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery Made Plain and Easy (1747), and various nineteenth-century domestic cookbooks. The defining features, a buttery dough flavoured with lemon, currants, and sometimes rosewater or caraway, have remained consistent across the documented recipe tradition. The recipe here follows the lemon-and-currant version documented in nineteenth-century sources.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled biscuits in a sealed bag. Thaw at room temperature for 30 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keep in a tin for up to five days. The biscuit firms further over the first day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 160°C. Bake until the edges are pale gold and the surface is just dry; the centre will still feel slightly soft when pressed. The biscuit crisps as it cools.

glossaryTerms.definition: A classic English butter biscuit, firmer and crisper than shortbread, made from flour, butter, sugar, egg, and flavourings — traditionally lemon zest, currants, and sometimes rosewater or caraway. The texture depends on the ratio: Shrewsbury biscuits use more egg and sugar than shortbread, which makes them crisper and less crumbly. Roll the dough cold and bake until just gold at the edges; overbaking makes them brittle rather than crisp.

content.text: sits between a shortbread and a snap: short from the butter but with more bite from the egg and sugar. The dough is forgiving: it does not overwork as easily as shortbread, and rolls cleanly when chilled. Lemon zest and currants are the most useful combination.

Beat the butter and sugar together until pale and fluffy. Beat in the egg and lemon zest. Add the flour, salt, and currants and mix to a firm dough — it will come together cleanly. Shape into a disc, wrap in cling film, and refrigerate for 20 minutes.

Heat the oven to 180°C conventional. Line two baking trays with baking paper. On a lightly floured surface, roll the dough to 5 mm thickness. Cut into rounds of 6 to 7 cm diameter and place on the trays, leaving a 2 cm gap between each. Bake for 12 to 14 minutes until the edges are pale gold and the surface is just dry. The centres will still feel slightly soft when pressed; the biscuits firm as they cool. Transfer to a rack after 3 minutes and cool completely before storing.
